symbols: halo around central figure — the divine glory of Christ as Pantocrator
fiery red and gold palette — divine energy, the consuming fire of God's presence
enthroned central figure — Christ as ruler and judge of all creation
surrounding figures — the faithful gathered around the divine presence
abstract, dissolving forms — the mystery of the divine that transcends material representation
description: A semi-abstract painting in warm reds, oranges, golds, and whites. A central figure with a golden halo is discernible, seated or enthroned, rendered in loose, fiery brushstrokes that dissolve into the surrounding space. To the right, a cluster of additional figures appears, also rendered in warm tones with hints of golden halos. The background is pale cream-white, making the warm-toned figures glow like flames. The style merges iconic tradition with modern expressionist abstraction.
theological_description: Tamas's Pantocrator draws on the ancient Byzantine iconographic tradition of Christ as 'Ruler of All' (Pantocrator) — a title expressing Christ's cosmic sovereignty over all creation. The Pantocrator icon, found in the dome of every Orthodox church, depicts Christ as described in Revelation: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega... who is, and who was, and who is to come, the Almighty' (Revelation 1:8). The fiery palette recalls the theophanic tradition — God appearing as fire to Moses in the burning bush (Exodus 3:2), as a pillar of fire to Israel (Exodus 13:21), and in Hebrews' declaration that 'our God is a consuming fire' (Hebrews 12:29). The semi-abstract style captures what icons have always attempted: not a photographic likeness but a window into the divine reality. The surrounding figures recall the heavenly liturgy of Revelation where 'the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb' (Revelation 5:8). Paul's Christological hymn declares that Christ is 'the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation. For in him all things were created' (Colossians 1:15-16) — this cosmic authority is what Pantocrator imagery proclaims.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ as Alpha and Omega, the Almighty — the theological foundation of the Pantocrator image
Christ as image of the invisible God and ruler over all creation
God as consuming fire — the fiery palette expressing divine majesty
The heavenly court worshipping before the enthroned Christ — the surrounding figures
